mission.title: Install and Configure RabbitMQ Cluster Operator
mission.description: The RabbitMQ Cluster Operator is a Kubernetes operator designed to manage RabbitMQ clusters within Kubernetes environments. It simplifies the deployment, scaling, and management of RabbitMQ instances, making it ideal for applications that require reliable messaging. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add the RabbitMQ Cluster Operator Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official RabbitMQ Cluster Operator Helm repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add rabbitmq https://rabbitmq.github.io/cluster-operator

```

> This allows you to install the operator using Helm.

steps.title: Install the RabbitMQ Cluster Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the RabbitMQ Cluster Operator using Helm, which manages the lifecycle of RabbitMQ clusters.

```bash

helm install rabbitmq-operator rabbitmq/cluster-operator --version 2.20.1 --namespace rabbitmq-system --create-namespace

```

> This installs the operator in a dedicated namespace.

steps.title: Deploy a RabbitMQ cluster
steps.description:
This step deploys a sample RabbitMQ cluster using a predefined configuration file.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rabbitmq/cluster-operator/main/docs/examples/hello-world/rabbitmq.yaml

```

> This creates a RabbitMQ cluster with default settings.

steps.title: Verify the RabbitMQ cluster is running
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the RabbitMQ pods to ensure they are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n rabbitmq-system

```

> Look for the status of the RabbitMQ pods to confirm they are in 'Running' state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the RabbitMQ Cluster Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the RabbitMQ Cluster Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this may result in data loss if persistent volumes are not retained.

```bash

helm uninstall rabbitmq-operator --namespace rabbitmq-system

kubectl delete namespace rabbitmq-system

```

> This command removes the operator.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the RabbitMQ Cluster Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup any important data. This step upgrades the operator to the specified version.

```bash

helm upgrade rabbitmq-operator rabbitmq/cluster-operator --version 2.20.1 --namespace rabbitmq-system

kubectl get pods -n rabbitmq-system

```

> This command upgrades the operator to the latest version.
> Verify that the pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to install CRD'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Custom Resource Definition (CRD) may not have been applied correctly.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRD using 'kubectl apply -f https://github.com/rabbitmq/cluster-operator/releases/latest/download/cluster-operator.yml'.

troubleshooting.title: RabbitMQ pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration or resource limits being exceeded.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n rabbitmq-system' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'RabbitMQ cluster not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The RabbitMQ cluster may not have enough resources allocated.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the cluster has sufficient CPU and memory resources allocated in the configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Permission denied'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** RBAC permissions may not be set correctly for the operator.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the service account used by the operator has the necessary permissions.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for RabbitMQ 4.1.3, improved CRD management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the RabbitMQ pods running with the output similar to 'NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE' followed by the pod names in the 'Running' state.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a running Kubernetes cluster with kubectl and Helm installed.
